title: Array to linked list
description: Convert an array into a singly linked list and return its head

examples.explanation: Step 1: Check array not empty. Step 2: Create head node with value 1, current = head. Step 3: For element 2: create new node(2), current.next = node(2), current = node(2). Step 4: For element 3: create new node(3), current.next = node(3), current = node(3). Step 5: Return head pointing to node(1). Final list: node(1).next = node(2), node(2).next = node(3), node(3).next = None.
Step 1: Check array empty, return None immediately. No nodes created.

approaches.name: Brute Force O(n) Iterative
approaches.summary: Iterate through the array from start to end, creating a new node for each element and linking them sequentially. This is the most straightforward method, building the list in a single pass.

ladders.title: Full Problem — Convert Array to Singly Linked List (Iterative Approach)
ladders.desc: Complete implementation of converting an array to a singly linked list using iterative node creation.
ladders.explanation: The intuition behind this approach is straightforward: we need to traverse the array once from beginning to end, creating a new node for each element and linking them together in the same order as the array elements. This works because arrays have sequential access, and linked lists are built by connecting nodes sequentially. We maintain two pointers: 'head' to remember the start of the list, and 'current' to track where to attach the next node. The algorithm starts by checking if the array is empty (return None). Then for each element in the array: 1) Create a new node with the element's value. 2) If it's the first node, set both head and current to this node. 3) Otherwise, link the current node's next to the new node, then move current to the new node. Time complexity is O(n) where n is array length, as we process each element once. Space complexity is O(n) for the new linked list nodes. This connects to L1 by using the node creation and linking logic as the key subroutine. Common mistakes include: forgetting to handle empty arrays (should return None), not updating the current pointer correctly (leading to lost nodes), and creating nodes with incorrect linking order. Real code pattern: def array_to_linked_list(arr): if not arr: return None; head = ListNode(arr[0]); current = head; for i in range(1, len(arr)): new_node = ListNode(arr[i]); current.next = new_node; current = new_node; return head.

examples.trace: Step 1: Check array not empty. Step 2: Create head node with value 1, current = head. Step 3: For element 2: create new node(2), current.next = node(2), current = node(2). Step 4: For element 3: create new node(3), current.next = node(3), current = node(3). Step 5: Return head pointing to node(1). Final list: node(1).next = node(2), node(2).next = node(3), node(3).next = None.
Step 1: Check array empty, return None immediately. No nodes created.

ladders.title: Key Sub-routine — Create and Link Nodes in Sequence
ladders.desc: Implement the core loop that creates nodes from array elements and links them together.
ladders.explanation: The intuition is that building a linked list from an array requires sequentially processing each element: create a node, then connect it to the previous node. This sub-routine focuses on the iterative linking process, assuming we already have the first node created. We need a 'current' pointer that starts at the first node, and for each subsequent array element: create a new node, set current.next to point to it, then move current to this new node. This works because linked lists grow by extending from the tail. The algorithm: 1) Initialize current = first_node. 2) For each element in array starting from index 1: create new_node with element value; set current.next = new_node; set current = new_node. Time O(n-1) for n elements (after first), space O(n-1) for new nodes. This feeds into L0 by being the main loop after handling the head. Common mistakes: starting loop at wrong index (should be 1, not 0), forgetting to update current pointer (causing only two nodes), or incorrectly linking (e.g., linking new_node to itself). Real code pattern: current = head; for val in arr[1:]: new_node = ListNode(val); current.next = new_node; current = new_node.

examples.trace: Step 1: current = node(1). Step 2: For element 2: create node(2), current.next = node(2), current = node(2). Step 3: For element 3: create node(3), current.next = node(3), current = node(3). Result: node(1).next = node(2), node(2).next = node(3).
Step 1: current = node(5). Step 2: Array empty, no loop iterations. Result: node(5).next remains None.

ladders.title: Core Logic — Sequential Traversal and Node Attachment
ladders.desc: Understand how to traverse data and attach new nodes to build a linear structure.
ladders.explanation: The intuition is that converting sequential data (like an array) to a linked list requires maintaining a 'tail' pointer where new nodes can be attached. This core logic involves two key operations: iterating through the source data, and for each item, attaching a new node to the current end of the list. It works because linked lists are dynamic structures where we can efficiently add to the end by keeping a reference to the last node. The algorithm: 1) Start with a list (initially empty or with one node). 2) For each item in the source: create a node with item value; if list empty, set head and tail to node; else set tail.next = node and update tail = node. Time O(n) for n items, space O(n) for nodes. This connects to L1 by providing the fundamental pattern of tail-based attachment. Common mistakes: losing the tail reference (need to update it), not handling empty list case, or attaching nodes in wrong order. Real code pattern: tail = None; for val in data: new_node = Node(val); if not head: head = new_node; tail = new_node; else: tail.next = new_node; tail = new_node.

examples.input: Data: [A, B, C], start with empty list
examples.trace: Step 1: For A: create node(A), head=node(A), tail=node(A). Step 2: For B: create node(B), tail.next=node(B), tail=node(B). Step 3: For C: create node(C), tail.next=node(C), tail=node(C). Final: node(A).next=node(B), node(B).next=node(C).
Step 1: For X: create node(X), head=node(X), tail=node(X). No further steps.

ladders.title: Building Block — Creating and Connecting Linked List Nodes
ladders.desc: Learn how to create individual nodes and link them using next pointers.
ladders.explanation: The intuition is that linked lists are built from nodes that contain data and a reference to the next node. This building block focuses on the basic operations of node creation and setting the 'next' pointer to form connections. It works because each node is an independent object, and linking them via 'next' creates the chain structure. The algorithm involves: 1) Defining a Node class with attributes for value and next. 2) Creating node instances with given values. 3) Connecting nodes by assigning node1.next = node2. Time O(1) per operation, space O(1) per node. This feeds into L2 by providing the components for sequential attachment. Common mistakes: not initializing next to None, incorrectly referencing nodes, or creating circular references. Real code pattern: class Node: def __init__(self, val): self.val = val; self.next = None; node1 = Node(1); node2 = Node(2); node1.next = node2.

examples.input: Create nodes with values 10 and 20, then link 10 to 20
examples.trace: Step 1: Create node10: val=10, next=None. Step 2: Create node20: val=20, next=None. Step 3: Set node10.next = node20. Result: node10 points to node20, node20.next is None.

examples.input: Create a single node with value 5
examples.trace: Step 1: Create node5: val=5, next=None. No linking needed.

ladders.title: Basic Operation — Iterating Through an Array
ladders.desc: Master the fundamental skill of traversing all elements in an array sequentially.
ladders.explanation: The intuition is that to process each element in an array, we need to access them one by one from first to last. This basic operation is crucial because arrays store elements in contiguous memory, and iteration allows us to handle each item. It works by using a loop (like for or while) with an index or direct element access. The algorithm: 1) Start at index 0. 2) While index < length of array: access array[index]; perform operation; increment index. Time O(n) for n elements, space O(1) if not storing elements. This connects to L3 by providing the traversal mechanism for node creation. Common mistakes: going out of bounds (index >= length), skipping elements, or using wrong loop conditions. Real code pattern: for i in range(len(arr)): element = arr[i]; or for element in arr: directly use element.

examples.trace: Step 1: i=0, arr[0]=3, print 3. Step 2: i=1, arr[1]=7, print 7. Step 3: i=2, arr[2]=2, print 2. Step 4: i=3, stop.
Step 1: i=0, arr[0]=10, print 10. Step 2: i=1, stop.

ladders.title: Concept Foundation — Understanding Arrays and Simple Data Storage
ladders.desc: Learn what an array is and how to store and access multiple values.
ladders.explanation: The intuition is that an array is a basic data structure that holds a collection of elements in a fixed order, accessible by index. This foundation is essential because arrays are the input for our problem, and understanding them is prerequisite to processing their elements. It works by allocating contiguous memory slots, each storing one value, with indices starting at 0. The concept involves: 1) Defining an array (e.g., [1, 2, 3]). 2) Accessing elements using indices (e.g., first element is at index 0). 3) Knowing properties like length (number of elements). Time O(1) for access by index, space O(n) to store n elements. This feeds into L4 by providing the data structure to iterate over. Common mistakes: confusing indices (starting at 1 instead of 0), misunderstanding array bounds, or not recognizing arrays as ordered sequences. Real code pattern: arr = [1, 2, 3]; first = arr[0]; length = len(arr).

examples.trace: Step 1: Array has 3 elements. Step 2: arr[0] = 5. Step 3: arr[1] = 10. Step 4: arr[2] = 15.
Step 1: Array has 1 element. Step 2: arr[0] = 8.

approaches.name: Recursive O(n) Backward Construction
approaches.summary: Use recursion to process the array from the end to the beginning, creating nodes and linking them in reverse order. This leverages the call stack to build the list from the tail up.

ladders.title: Full Problem — Recursive Backward Construction of Linked List from Array
ladders.desc: Complete solution using recursion to build a singly linked list from an array by processing elements from end to beginning.
ladders.explanation: This approach leverages the natural LIFO (Last-In-First-Out) property of recursion to construct a linked list from the tail upward. The intuition is that when we reach the end of the array (base case), we return None (null pointer), representing the end of the list. As each recursive call returns, we create a new node with the current array element and link it to the previously built sublist (which is the tail portion). This backward linking ensures that the first array element becomes the head of the final list. The algorithm starts with index 0 and recursively processes increasing indices until reaching the end. Each step creates a node whose 'next' pointer points to the result of the recursive call for the next index. This builds the list in reverse order relative to the recursion unwinding. Time complexity is O(n) where n is array length, as each element is processed once. Space complexity is O(n) due to recursion stack depth and node storage. This connects to L1 by implementing the complete recursive function. Common mistakes include incorrect base case handling (off-by-one errors), forgetting to return the head, or mishandling empty arrays. The pattern demonstrates elegant use of recursion for backward construction.

examples.trace:
Step 1: Call convert(arr, 0) where arr=[1,2,3], i=0
Step 2: i=0 < len=3, so call convert(arr, 1)
Step 3: i=1 < 3, call convert(arr, 2)
Step 4: i=2 < 3, call convert(arr, 3)
Step 5: i=3 == len=3, base case returns None
Step 6: Back to i=2: create node with arr[2]=3, node.next = None (from step 5), return node(3)
Step 7: Back to i=1: create node with arr[1]=2, node.next = node(3) (from step 6), return node(2)
Step 8: Back to i=0: create node with arr[0]=1, node.next = node(2) (from step 7), return node(1)
Step 9: Final list: 1->2->3->None

Step 1: Call convert(arr, 0) where arr=[], i=0
Step 2: i=0 == len=0, base case returns None immediately
Step 3: Final list: None (empty list)

ladders.title: Key Sub-routine — Recursive Node Creation with Index Tracking
ladders.desc: The recursive function that processes array elements and creates linked list nodes.
ladders.explanation: This sub-routine is the heart of the approach: a recursive function that takes an array and current index as parameters, and returns the head of the sublist starting from that index. The intuition is divide-and-conquer: at each step, we handle the current element and delegate the rest of the array to a recursive call. The base case occurs when index equals array length (we've processed all elements), returning None to signify end of list. In the recursive case, we make a recursive call for the next index, which builds the tail portion of the list. Then we create a new node with the current array element, set its next pointer to the result of the recursive call (the already-built tail), and return this node. This creates backward linking as recursion unwinds. Time complexity is O(n) as each index triggers one recursive call. Space complexity is O(n) for recursion stack. This connects to L0 as the complete solution wraps this function. Common mistakes include not updating index correctly, infinite recursion from missing base case, or not linking nodes properly. The pattern shows clean recursion with index parameter.

examples.trace:
Step 1: Call func(arr, 0), arr=[4,5], i=0
Step 2: i=0 < len=2, so call func(arr, 1)
Step 3: i=1 < 2, call func(arr, 2)
Step 4: i=2 == len=2, base case returns None
Step 5: Back to i=1: create node(5), node.next = None, return node(5)
Step 6: Back to i=0: create node(4), node.next = node(5), return node(4)
Step 7: Result: 4->5->None

Step 1: Call func(arr, 0), arr=[9], i=0
Step 2: i=0 < len=1, call func(arr, 1)
Step 3: i=1 == len=1, base case returns None
Step 4: Back to i=0: create node(9), node.next = None, return node(9)
Step 5: Result: 9

ladders.title: Core Logic — Backward Linking via Recursion Unwinding
ladders.desc: The technique of creating nodes and linking them in reverse order as recursive calls return.
ladders.explanation: This core logic captures the essential insight: we build the linked list from tail to head by leveraging the recursion call stack. The intuition is that recursion naturally processes information in reverse order when unwinding. During the recursive descent, we reach the end of the array (base case). As we return from each recursive call, we're effectively moving backward through the array. At each return step, we create a node for the current array element and link it to the already-constructed tail (which contains all subsequent elements). This means the last array element becomes the last node in the list, and the first array element becomes the head. The linking happens in the opposite direction of array traversal. Time complexity for this logic is O(1) per node creation, but overall O(n). Space complexity includes O(n) for recursion stack. This connects to L1 as it's implemented within the recursive function. Common mistakes include trying to link forward during descent or not preserving the returned head. The pattern demonstrates how recursion's LIFO behavior enables backward construction.

examples.input: Processing element 2 in array [1,2,3] after recursive call returns node(3)
examples.trace:
Step 1: After base case returns None for index 3
Step 2: At index 2: recursive call returned node(3) (which points to None)
Step 3: Create new node with value arr[2]=2
Step 4: Set node(2).next = node(3) (the returned sublist)
Step 5: Now node(2) points to node(3), forming 2->3->None
Step 6: Return node(2) to previous call

examples.input: Processing element 1 in array [1,2] after recursive call returns node(2)
examples.trace:
Step 1: After base case returns None for index 2
Step 2: At index 1: recursive call returned node(2) (2->None)
Step 3: Create new node with value arr[1]=1
Step 4: Set node(1).next = node(2)
Step 5: Now node(1) points to node(2), forming 1->2->None
Step 6: Return node(1) as final head

ladders.title: Building Block — Recursive Function with Base Case and Recursive Case
ladders.desc: Implementing a recursive function that handles termination condition and self-call.
ladders.explanation: This building block focuses on the structure of a recursive function, which is fundamental to the approach. The intuition is that recursion solves problems by breaking them into smaller, similar subproblems. A recursive function must have: 1) A base case that stops the recursion (when the problem is trivial), and 2) A recursive case that calls itself with modified parameters. For array-to-list conversion, the base case is when we've processed all elements (index equals array length). The recursive case processes the current element and calls itself for the next index. The function returns a node pointer, building up the result as calls return. Time complexity depends on number of recursive calls (O(n)). Space complexity includes stack frames (O(n)). This connects to L2 as it provides the framework for backward linking. Common mistakes include missing base case (causing infinite recursion), incorrect recursive parameters, or not returning values properly. The pattern teaches how to design recursive solutions with clear termination and progression.

examples.input: Function to process array [1, 2, 3] from index i, array of length=3
examples.trace:
Step 1: Define function f(arr, i)
Step 2: Base case: if i == len(arr): return None
Step 3: Recursive case: else: rest = f(arr, i+1)  # Process rest
Step 4: Create node with arr[i]
Step 5: node.next = rest
Step 6: Return node
Step 7: Example call f([1,2,3], 0):
  - i=0: call f(arr,1)
  - i=1: call f(arr,2)
  - i=2: call f(arr,3)
  - i=3: base case returns None
  - Unwind as described in L2

Step 1: Define function f(arr, i)
Step 2: Base case: if i == 1: return None
Step 3: For arr=[5], i=0: call f(arr,1)
Step 4: i=1: base case returns None
Step 5: Back to i=0: create node(5), node.next = None, return node(5)

testCases.expected: Recursive call for i=1, then base case at i=2
Recursive call for i=1, then base case
Recursive call for i=3, then base case at i=4
Recursive calls for i=1,2,3,4, base case at i=5
Recursive call for i=2, then base case
Recursive calls for i=1,2, base case at i=3

ladders.title: Basic Operation — Creating and Linking Singly Linked List Nodes
ladders.desc: Fundamental operation of instantiating a node object and setting its next pointer.
ladders.explanation: This basic operation involves creating individual linked list nodes and establishing connections between them. The intuition is that a singly linked list is a chain of nodes, where each node contains data and a reference (pointer) to the next node. To create a node, we allocate memory for a structure with two fields: 'val' (the data value) and 'next' (pointer to next node). Linking nodes involves setting the 'next' pointer of one node to point to another node object. In the recursive approach, we create nodes one at a time and link each new node to the previously constructed sublist. The 'next' pointer of the last node is set to None to indicate list termination. Time complexity for node creation and linking is O(1) per node. Space complexity is O(1) per node plus references. This connects to L3 as nodes are created within the recursive function. Common mistakes include forgetting to set 'next' pointers, creating nodes with incorrect values, or not handling None properly. The pattern is essential for any linked list manipulation.

examples.input: Create node with value 10 and link to existing node with value 20
examples.trace:
Step 1: Create new_node = Node(val=10)
Step 2: Set new_node.next = existing_node (where existing_node.val=20)
Step 3: Result: new_node points to existing_node
Step 4: If existing_node was 20->None, now 10->20->None

examples.input: Create node with value 5 and link to None
examples.trace:
Step 1: Create new_node = Node(val=5)
Step 2: Set new_node.next = None
Step 3: Result: 5->None (a single-node list)

ladders.title: Concept Foundation — Understanding Recursion and Linked List Nodes
ladders.desc: Prerequisite concepts: what is recursion and what is a linked list node.
ladders.explanation: This foundation covers two essential concepts. First, recursion: a function that calls itself to solve smaller instances of the same problem. It must have a base case (stopping condition) to prevent infinite calls. When a recursive function calls itself, the current state is saved on the call stack, and a new instance executes. As calls return, they unwind, allowing completion of each instance. Second, a linked list node: a basic building block of linked lists. In a singly linked list, each node contains data (e.g., an integer) and a 'next' pointer/reference to the next node in sequence. The last node's 'next' is None (or null), marking the list's end. Nodes are dynamically allocated and connected via pointers. Together, these concepts enable the recursive approach: recursion processes the array, and nodes form the list structure. Time and space considerations arise from recursive calls and node allocations. This connects to L4 as it underpins node operations. Common beginner mistakes include not understanding stack behavior in recursion or confusion about pointer vs. value. The pattern introduces fundamental programming concepts.

examples.input: Simple recursive function to count down from n
examples.trace:
Step 1: Define count(n): if n<=0 return; else print(n); count(n-1)
Step 2: Call count(3)
Step 3: n=3>0, print 3, call count(2)
Step 4: n=2>0, print 2, call count(1)
Step 5: n=1>0, print 1, call count(0)
Step 6: n=0<=0, return
Step 7: Unwind: returns to count(1), then count(2), then count(3)
Step 8: Output: 3,2,1

Step 1: Define Node class with val and next attributes
Step 2: Create node_b = Node(val=20, next=None)
Step 3: Create node_a = Node(val=10, next=node_b)
Step 4: Now node_a.val=10, node_a.next points to node_b
Step 5: node_b.val=20, node_b.next=None
Step 6: This forms list: 10->20->None

testCases.input: Recursive call: f(2) where f(n)=n if n<=1 else f(n-1)+f(n-2)
Recursive function with base case at n=0
Node with val=100, next pointing to another node

approaches.name: Dummy Head O(n) Iterative
approaches.summary: Use a dummy head node to simplify the linking process while iterating through the array, avoiding special cases for the first node. This enhances code clarity and reduces edge-case handling.

ladders.title: Full Problem — Array to Linked List with Dummy Head
ladders.desc: Complete implementation converting an array to a singly linked list using dummy head technique
ladders.explanation: The dummy head approach elegantly solves the array-to-linked-list conversion by eliminating special handling for the first node. We create a temporary dummy node that serves as a placeholder before the actual head of our linked list. As we iterate through the array, we create new nodes for each element and link them sequentially. The dummy node's next pointer always points to the current tail of our growing linked list, making the linking process uniform for all nodes. This technique is particularly valuable because it removes the need for conditional logic to check if we're creating the first node. The intuition is that by having a consistent starting point (the dummy node), we can treat every node addition identically, which simplifies code logic and reduces bugs. The algorithm proceeds as follows: 1) Create a dummy node with any value (typically 0 or None). 2) Maintain a 'current' pointer that starts at the dummy node. 3) Iterate through each element in the input array. 4) For each element, create a new node with that value. 5) Link the current node's next pointer to this new node. 6) Move the current pointer to this new node. 7) After processing all elements, return dummy.next as the actual head of the linked list. Time complexity is O(n) where n is the array length, as we process each element once. Space complexity is O(n) for the n nodes created. This connects directly to the broader concept of linked list construction patterns, where dummy heads are a common technique to simplify edge cases. Common mistakes include forgetting to return dummy.next (returning dummy instead), not updating the current pointer correctly, or mishandling empty arrays. The pattern ensures that even with empty input, we correctly return None (via dummy.next).

examples.trace:
Step 1: Create dummy node (value=0, next=None). Current points to dummy.
Step 2: Process element 1: Create node1 (value=1). dummy.next = node1. Current moves to node1.
Step 3: Process element 2: Create node2 (value=2). node1.next = node2. Current moves to node2.
Step 4: Process element 3: Create node3 (value=3). node2.next = node3. Current moves to node3.
Step 5: Return dummy.next which points to node1. Final list: 1→2→3→None.

Step 1: Create dummy node (value=0, next=None). Current points to dummy.
Step 2: Array is empty, so no iterations occur.
Step 3: Return dummy.next which is None. Final list: None.

ladders.title: Key Sub-routine — Iterative Node Linking with Dummy Head
ladders.desc: The core linking logic that connects nodes while traversing the array
ladders.explanation: This sub-routine represents the heart of the dummy head approach: the iterative process of creating and linking nodes. The key insight is maintaining two pointers: the dummy head (which never moves) and a current pointer that tracks the last node in the growing list. For each array element, we perform three consistent operations: create a new node with the element's value, link it to the current tail, and advance the current pointer. The dummy head serves as an anchor that gives us a consistent starting point for the first real node. The intuition is that by having a fixed reference point (dummy), we can build the list forward without needing to check if we're at the beginning. The algorithm: 1) Initialize current = dummy. 2) For each value in array: a) Create new_node with value. b) Set current.next = new_node. c) Set current = new_node. 3) Return dummy.next. Time complexity is O(n) for the loop. Space complexity is O(1) for pointers plus O(n) for the nodes (but that's considered separate from the linking logic). This feeds into the full problem by providing the essential linking mechanism. Common mistakes include updating current before linking (which breaks the chain), or not handling the empty array case properly (though dummy.next handles it). The pattern demonstrates how dummy heads simplify linked list construction by making every node addition identical.

examples.trace:
Initial: dummy (value=0, next=None), current = dummy
Step 1: Value 4: Create node4. current.next = node4. current = node4.
Step 2: Value 5: Create node5. current.next = node5. current = node5.
Step 3: Value 6: Create node6. current.next = node6. current = node6.
Final: dummy.next points to node4, forming 4→5→6→None.

Initial: dummy (value=0, next=None), current = dummy
Step 1: Value 9: Create node9. current.next = node9. current = node9.
Final: dummy.next points to node9, forming 9→None.

ladders.title: Core Logic — Dummy Head Technique for Simplified Linking
ladders.desc: Understanding how a dummy node eliminates special cases in linked list construction
ladders.explanation: The dummy head technique is a powerful pattern that simplifies linked list operations by providing a consistent starting point. The core insight is that by creating a temporary node before the actual head, we can treat the first real node identically to all subsequent nodes. This eliminates the need for conditional checks like 'if head is None: head = new_node else: current.next = new_node'. The intuition is that the dummy node acts as a placeholder that maintains a reference to the beginning of the list while we build it. As we add nodes, we always work with current.next, where current starts at dummy and moves along the list. This ensures uniform code for all node insertions. The technique works because: 1) The dummy node's next pointer initially points to None. 2) When we add the first real node, we set dummy.next to it. 3) For subsequent nodes, we're simply extending the chain from the last node. 4) Finally, we return dummy.next, which points to the actual first node (or None if empty). Time complexity for the technique itself is O(1) per operation, but it enables O(n) construction. Space complexity is O(1) for the dummy node. This feeds into the linking sub-routine by providing the structural foundation. Common mistakes include modifying the dummy node's value (irrelevant), or forgetting that dummy should be separate from the actual list. The pattern is widely used in linked list problems to handle edge cases cleanly.

examples.input: Building list from [2, 3] with and without dummy head
examples.trace:
Without dummy: Step 1: Check if head is None (yes), so head = node2. Step 2: Check if head is None (no), so find tail and set node2.next = node3.
With dummy: Step 1: dummy.next = node2, current = node2. Step 2: current.next = node3, current = node3. Both give 2→3→None, but dummy version has no conditionals.

Without dummy: head starts as None, remains None, return None.
With dummy: dummy.next starts as None, remains None after loop, return dummy.next which is None. Dummy approach has no special empty case handling.

ladders.title: Building Block — Singly Linked List Node Creation and Linking
ladders.desc: Creating individual nodes and connecting them via next pointers
ladders.explanation: This building block focuses on the fundamental operations of creating a linked list node and setting its next pointer. A singly linked list node typically has two attributes: a value (storing data) and a next pointer (reference to the next node). The intuition is that each node is an independent object that becomes part of a chain through these pointers. Creating a node involves allocating memory and initializing its value and next pointer (usually to None). Linking nodes means setting one node's next pointer to reference another node, forming a sequence. This is the atomic operation of linked list construction. The algorithm for creating and linking: 1) Define a Node class with value and next attributes. 2) To create a node: node = Node(value). 3) To link node A to node B: A.next = B. 4) The last node's next should be None to indicate end of list. Time complexity for creation and linking is O(1) per node. Space complexity is O(1) per node plus the node's value. This feeds into the dummy head technique by providing the basic elements that are manipulated. Common mistakes include not initializing next to None, circular references (A.next = A), or losing references by overwriting pointers incorrectly. Understanding this block is essential because linked lists are built entirely from these simple operations.

examples.input: Create nodes for values 7 and 8, then link
examples.trace:
Step 1: Create node7: value=7, next=None.
Step 2: Create node8: value=8, next=None.
Step 3: Link: node7.next = node8.
Result: node7 points to node8, node8.next is None.

examples.input: Create three nodes 1, 2, 3 and link sequentially
examples.trace:
Step 1: Create node1: value=1, next=None.
Step 2: Create node2: value=2, next=None.
Step 3: Create node3: value=3, next=None.
Step 4: Link node1 to node2: node1.next = node2.
Step 5: Link node2 to node3: node2.next = node3.
Result: Chain from node1 to node3.

testCases.input: Link node A to B where A=Node(1), B=Node(2)
What is next of last node?
Create node with value 100 and next=None

ladders.title: Basic Operation — Array Traversal and Element Access
ladders.desc: Iterating through an array and retrieving each element sequentially
ladders.explanation: This basic operation involves sequentially accessing each element in an array, which is fundamental to converting the array to a linked list. The intuition is that an array stores elements in contiguous memory locations with indices, and we need to visit each one exactly once to create corresponding linked list nodes. The process is straightforward: start from the first element (index 0) and proceed to the last element (index n-1), performing an operation on each. The algorithm: 1) Get the length of the array (n). 2) Use a loop (for or while) that runs from i=0 to i<n-1. 3) In each iteration, access array[i] to get the current element. 4) Perform desired operation (like creating a node). Time complexity is O(n) for traversing n elements. Space complexity is O(1) for the iteration variables. This feeds into node creation by providing the values to store in nodes. Common mistakes include off-by-one errors (going beyond n-1), modifying the array during traversal (if not intended), or using inefficient access patterns. For linked list conversion, we need this traversal to feed values into the node creation process. The pattern is essential because it's how we transfer data from the array structure to the linked list structure.

examples.trace:
Step 1: i=0, access array[0]=10
Step 2: i=1, access array[1]=20
Step 3: i=2, access array[2]=30
Step 4: i=3, stop (since length=3)

Step 1: i=0, access array[0]=5
Step 2: i=1, stop (length=1)

ladders.title: Concept Foundation — What is a Linked List Node?
ladders.desc: Understanding the basic unit of a linked list: a node with value and next pointer
ladders.explanation: A linked list node is the fundamental building block of a linked list data structure. Think of it as a container that holds two things: a piece of data (called the value) and a reference to the next container in the sequence (called the next pointer). The intuition is similar to a treasure hunt where each clue points to the location of the next clue. The node's value can be any data type (integer, string, etc.), and the next pointer is either a reference to another node or None (meaning end of the list). This simple structure allows nodes to be connected in a chain, forming a linked list. Unlike arrays where elements are stored in contiguous memory, linked list nodes can be scattered in memory, connected only by these pointers. The node concept is crucial because every linked list operation ultimately manipulates nodes. Creating a node involves allocating memory for it and setting its initial value and next pointer. The node doesn't know about the entire list; it only knows its own data and who comes next. This foundation feeds into all higher-level linked list operations. Common misunderstandings include thinking nodes store indices (they don't), or confusing the node with the entire list. For beginners, visualize a node as a box with two compartments: one for data, one for the address of the next box.

examples.trace:
Step 1: Allocate memory for a new node object.
Step 2: Set the node's value attribute to 42.
Step 3: Set the node's next attribute to None (default).
Result: A standalone node containing 42, not pointing to anything else.

examples.input: Two nodes: one with value 5, one with value 10
examples.trace:
Step 1: Create nodeA: value=5, next=None.
Step 2: Create nodeB: value=10, next=None.
Step 3: They are separate; nodeA doesn't know about nodeB unless we link them.
Result: Two nodes exist but aren't connected.

testCases.input: Is a node the same as a linked list?
How many nodes in an empty list?
What is the next of a new node?
Can a node point to itself?
